notes.note: Iudices: the standing court for parricide trials (quaestio inter sicarios) was composed of senators in this period; Sulla's reforms of 81 BC had restored the courts to senatorial control.
An Umbrian town some 90 km north of Rome, in the upper Tiber valley; modern Amelia.

Parricidium: the killing of one's father, or by extension any close kin. The traditional Roman penalty was the 'sack' (poena cullei): the convicted parricide was sewn into a leather sack with a dog, a cock, a viper, and a monkey, and thrown into a river.

Caecilia Metella, daughter of Q. Caecilius Metellus Balearicus (consul 123 BC) and sister of Q. Caecilius Metellus Nepos (consul 98 BC). A woman of the highest standing, sheltering Roscius at no small political risk.

Sulla, having freed thousands of slaves of the proscribed (the 10,000 Cornelii), wielded a personal army of clients owing him their freedom; Chrysogonus was the most influential of them and the chief manager of the proscription auctions.

The town councillors of an Italian municipium; the local senate. The decuriones of Ameria sent an embassy to Sulla on Roscius's behalf, but were intercepted by Chrysogonus before they could see the dictator.
An estate of six million sesterces sold for two thousand: a 3000-fold loss. Cicero's number is rhetorical-precise; the absurdity of the sale price is the point.
